justification: All pass criteria met: growth projection calculated (20% MoM × 12 months → ~8.9x, with specific numbers like 445K orders/day and 5 orders/sec); database identified as primary bottleneck (70% CPU, full table scans); 85% disk utilization flagged as critical/immediate emergency (6-10 weeks to full); index fix identified as highest ROI first step; clear phased plan (Phase 1: indexes/caching → Phase 2: read replicas/partitioning → Phase 3: strategic evolution); sharding explicitly deferred ('DO NOT attempt microservice decomposition', sharding mentioned only as month 18+ consideration); trade-offs include replication lag and cache invalidation complexity.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed growth math with specific timelines for CPU (8 weeks), disk (6-10 weeks), and API (month 3) exhaustion
Correctly identifies full table scans as root cause and indexes as highest-ROI fix
Disk emergency treated as IMMEDIATE action item with 'expand disk TODAY' urgency
Phasing is well-sequenced by effort-to-impact ratio, appropriate for 4-engineer team constraint
Explicitly warns against over-engineering: no microservices, no sharding, no distributed DB migration
Practical implementation details like CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, pgBouncer, pg_partman show operational awareness
Trade-offs are thorough — covers replication lag, cache invalidation, partitioning migration complexity, and connection exhaustion
Low-confidence areas and assumptions are honest and well-calibrated
Disk timeline estimate of 6-10 weeks is slightly generous — at 85% full with 20% monthly data growth, it could fill in as few as 4-5 weeks depending on data growth patterns
Write volume calculation (5 orders/sec) is correct but could have been more explicitly compared against PostgreSQL write throughput limits to strengthen the single-writer rigidity argument
